speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 21st of March, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. Rolling, and camera is ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in Darwin in 1975, after the Cyclone Tracy hit the town. They brought me there because I was the only person in Australia at the time with a diploma in community development. That was very new in these-- that's 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you earned that in Israel? Or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Hebrew University. And I was multilingual. And I was used to remote area living. That was a condition of the employment. Had a very good job. I never sat in the office. I went out. Because community development is that you identify the needs. Not like a social worker, from person to person. But community development is identifying the needs of a community. And because three people already are a community-- I'm telling this for your benefit-- so it is easier to identify common needs. That's why it is called community development. And I was extremely successful. And I invented, very quickly, how to go about it, that I did public opinion polls. I recruited quite a lot of people who did it for me. I designed the questionnaire. How-- the idea was to feedback the design team, how they would like to see the new Darwin. And so we did one survey after the other. They made a play about me. If they found the fat lady who will be in it and asked people the most impertinent questions. And the Darwin Players played the play. And I was quite a character. No Jews in Darwin. I always-- but-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --Sir Asher Joel, who has the newspaper in Mount-- in Mount Isa-- uh-huh, I'm not in Mount Isa yet. Well, they declared me redundant with a few others. And I got $20,000 redundancy money. And I never had that sort of money in my life before. That was in 1978. And somebody told me there is a tourist souvenir shop to sell in Mount Isa. So I thought to myself, well, I have a look at it, which I did. It belonged to a fellow with which I am still very friendly with. It was called Mike's Novelties. We changed it to Kitty's Novelties. I gave him that as a deposit. It was $50,000, and the rest to pay over three years. And there were copper spoons from the copper city from Taiwan. There were boots from China. Everything from somewhere else. And it was so boring, the old ladies would come in for a copper spoon. They had enough money from their tourist bus to buy a copper spoon and next door to the Cypriot-- Greek Cypriot, a green apple. That's all they could manage. They were old pensioners. I thought I'll go mental. And I had a gay friend, a very good one, and American. And he was in the drug business. And he had an encyclopedia for plants.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, Kitty, I'll give you, in consignment, a dozen encyclopedias. And you can sell them and try to sell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: People came from Darwin to buy these encyclopedias, because they all had plants in their backyard. I did not know what I was selling. I knew I bought it for 10. I sold it for 20. And that was very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I travelled to Sydney. Ron Resnick was his name, a good friend of mine, very unhappy. Diabetic and his homosexuality was not all that marvelous. And Bobby, his boyfriend, so they introduced me to another gay couple. And I had to go urgently to the toilet. And there was a toilet paper which had written on it Fraser. All along. Fraser, Fraser, Fraser. I said, how much does that cost? Can you just explain who Fraser is, for our America audience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the American audience. See, this is our-- was a Prime Minister, very hated by me because I am a Labor voter. And he's just on the opposite side of the spectrum. And that was this toilet paper. And I lived in Mount Isa, a strictly National Party country and environment. And-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had this--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --toilet paper. And they said to me, Kitty, for $0.50 you can have it. We'll give you a gross because nobody in Sydney wants it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's old hat. And we will help you. Because adult jokes is a big thing. And why don't you introduce adult jokes into Mount Isa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, I try with this mount-- Fraser roll. And they sent-- I took with me carton. A gross of Fraser roll. And I made my girl-- who is in Tasmania, and we are still very friendly, Helena. Helena made a pyramid in the window. And there was Fraser, Fraser, Fraser, everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the leader of the National Country Party, Mr. Bertoni, came in 10 minutes later. He said, Kitty, how many of these rolls have you got? I said, a gross. How much are they? $4.95. He took out a check and bought them all. And he didn't let me sign anything that I can't have them anymore. So I put them-- I telephone Sydney, the boys, to send me another gross. People from Darwin came for the Fraser rolls. So I realized adult jokes is big business, to make people laugh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe in hard porn. If that's what, then, he's waiting for, he won't get it. But I had blow-up dolls. They had three holes. I never could work out what for. And then I had little panties which had written 'Never on Sunday.' And then I had little hand-crocheted Peter heaters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day a man came. I was open on Sunday. He said, do you know where I am from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. He said, I'm from Kingaroy. That is the residence of the Premiere of-- of Queensland. A very reactionary, right-wing old man. And he said, I'm next door to old Joe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, would you give him a little package for me? He said, of course I will. I like your shop. You don't see these things in Queensland. Can you go to jail for it? I said, yes, it's a criminal offense. But they have to find me guilty. And so he-- I-- he picked a bright red Peter heater. And I sit at the typewriter and I wrote, 'The nights is getting colder and you are getting older. So what could be neater than a little Peter heater?' And I send it to Bjelke-Petersen. And two days later, the telephone went. And the lady said, Mrs. Fisher?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Premiere sends his compliments. He never laughed so much in 50 years. So I thought if the Premiere can laugh, I do better. I go to Hong Kong. Did not know where to go, what to do. I went into Chongqing chambers [? terribly, ?] [? nights ?] [? on the ?] [? road ?] where no thinking human being goes. But I used to go there before from New Guinea. And then I ask somebody discreetly where I could find a sex shop. And they said that's not allowed in Hong Kong, but adult jokes, yes. And that is in the-- in a Holiday Inn arcade, in the basement. Adult shops. I went in there. Chinese, very arrogant. Very unsympatico.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you know, we have just received a-- a shipment of stink bombs. I said, what's stink bomb? He said, it's fantastic. You step on it and it stinks out a-- I said, how much is this? So he told me a ridiculous price. I said, give me a gross.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only was working in the-- so 144 stink bombs. And then I-- I had took with me two empty suitcases. Nothing, only what I had on me, and I packed it all full of what I bought there. A little-- a little-- with match boxes. And they said, 'I love sex.' And when you open it, and a huge penis jumps out. And they were roaring, the yahoos, the cowboys came from the outside stations. They knew I was open. They came on trucks. They bought the shop out. Oh, there was a nipple for adults. And there was a-- a little-- and the rubber part was like a penis. And one day, in comes the police with sirens. Three cars of police. Mrs. Fisher, search warrant. You are running a business of improper conduct. I said, you have to prove it, gentlemen. You be careful. And they started to look and they didn't find anything. Because I had-- I had-- --badges, hundreds of badges. I had vibrators, very nice, in about three sizes. I had condoms which are put on a string. Colored condoms, beautiful ones, in the middle of the window. And Sir Asher Joel, every time he came through Mount Isa, they rang me from the mine. Mrs. Fisher, Sir Asher is on the way. Put on the kettle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Sir Asher came, put on his glasses. I want to see what you have on your shelves here. And he was peering around. And he said, you have a chutzpah to put things on your shelves which no decent human being would have. But you are very much loved here. Why do you tell everybody you are Jewish? I said, well, shall I tell them I'm Muslim? He said, no. But is it necessary? I said, what a hypocrite you are. I said you are-- here you are, that tall poster of the Jewish community, and everywhere. And you were knighted as a Jew. And I am not allowed to be a Jew? He said, yes, but it must be very difficult for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He felt-- he felt uncomfortable that I lived amongst these very ordinary goyim. There were a lot of German miners there. And when I came into the pub, every day I came into the German club, the only place I wanted to go. At 6:30 I shut the shop. I went in there. And for $0.80, I bought a bitter lemon. The miners were drinking beer. And they nudged each other. And they said, that's how the Jews are getting rich. Because they're drinking water. But when they went on leave, on holiday, they only bought off me. I must say, they were only buying off me. So the thing came to court. I started to sell little gadget-- little pipes for pot smoking. I got a connection somewhere. Very nice two gay boys. I was so involved in the gay community. They adopted me, somehow. And-- and then I had all these gadgets, which I'm-- which the police bought, purchased of me, so they can tell when people were using them of who sold them. because I am allowed to sell them. That's the law. But you are not allowed to use them. I knew all this when they took me to court. And I won. Because I said I'm selling novelties. And you prove to me that this is not a novelty in Mount Isa. And I won. And they had to pay me damages. And it was all over the newspapers. Sir Asher came. Said, Kitty, the [? Jewish ?] [INAUDIBLE] are coming. I will-- Jet Set Air are paying your fair. We're taking you to Melbourne for a nice Yiddish holiday. Where-- to get out of this schmutz. I told him I don't sell any schmutz. I sell adult jokes. And I get more-- I had little after-sex towels. Very sweet, each one had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitty, I-- I have a question about all this. How do you see your Jewish identity now, being a sole Jewish proprietor in Mount Isa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I see myself as Kitty Fisher. And the-- the rest are the other Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that mean you've made your own personal Jewishness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. But I can get very nice. When it comes to holidays I can get very-- they send me to the [INAUDIBLE]. to [INAUDIBLE]. I stayed in his house. Yom Kippur, they watched me, that you're not allowed to have a shower. Only the tip of your-- of your fingers. I said, I'd never seen a dirtier race in my life. And I went-- it was on a Shabbos, the Yom Kippur. I don't go to synagogue. So I-- I don't fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went and bought myself the Saturday paper. Because I thought the world will collapse if I don't read the Saturday

speaker: interviewee
text: All these [? Haganah ?] saw me marching with the newspaper home. [NON-ENGLISH], they call it [? 'Hot Ham ?] [? Street.' ?] They laugh at him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, you know how many sins you committed? No. I said, you were not in shul. You carried money. And you are-- you bought a newspaper of a goy. I said, well, if I was in Jerusalem, I would buy a newspaper of a Jew. Does that make the sin smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you wouldn't buy a newspaper in Israel on Yom Kippur. I said, well, I would in the old city, I bet you, anytime. And you know, they loved me so much. I was such an object for reform. I was-- no, the stink bombs in Mount Isa, in the movies, in the pictures, you see, I sold them for $2 each. I think I paid tuppence for them. And with all this nonsense, they accepted me. They thought I was meshuga. That is a fact. Because I am a diagnosed manic depressive. I have grave health problems. It all sounds so very nice when I tell you. But in the mornings I am not so wonderful. I suffer from depression. If I had depression today, you wouldn't be here because I wouldn't open the door. And I'm on a drug called lithium carbonate, which is a mood stabilizer, so that I don't go up and down and up and down, but a little bit like this. But then this is probably very Jewish, to have the swinging moods. And it doesn't make me unhappy all, the old memories--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which I'm having. Because I met an awful lot of friends. And all these cards you see around here, they are all people who remember me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when there is a bomb in Tel Aviv or in Netanya or somewhere else, I get telephone calls from Mount Isa and from Darwin, from people whom I met from the Gold Coast who retired. Kitty, are you all right? I am sitting here very gratefully, I must say, to the [INAUDIBLE] that they gave me against terrible protestation from the residents.

speaker: interviewee
text: The residents did not want me here. I was institutionalized. I was in lunatic asylums. I was-- everybody knew-- Everybody thought I was mad. But nobody knew how or why, or whether it was crazy or whether it was only depressive, or whether it was only loneliness, or whether it was Jewish, or whether it was reactive. Nobody can say. Only the German government pays me 907 deutsche mark a month. And now I'm asking for an increase, that I have so deteriorated in my health. And I have done so once before. And they nearly wrote that I had recovered. Because a psychiatrist, a Dr. [INAUDIBLE] in Saint George Hospital, never doubted for a minute that I-- that I am sick, that the depression is there. I mean, after all, he knew how to make a diagnosis. But he thought I made a remarkable recovery from all the-- my medical history, my files are about that thick, from all the times I was in hospital. Because I didn't function. I had to give the shop up in Mount Isa. And I have no money from it because I just had to leave. I couldn't-- I was getting worse and worse. The climate got me down. So now I'm under a first-class psychiatrist. And I am helpful. I work in the [INAUDIBLE] Hostel for derelict man, where I am very loved and where I do a lot of counseling, and where I do a lot of community development to help them to integrate into the work force, which is not very easy when you say your present address. But then, when I was sitting here in the north side clinic and they asked me what is your-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What is your-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --domicile? Where are you domiciled? They had to write itinerant. I had no home. If the [INAUDIBLE] would have not come forward and given me this for the duration of my life, it's not mine, for $120 a month on which I can exist on the pension, I would've slept under bridges, I guess. So somewhere, somehow, something, as you see, without me doing anything about it, happens to me from somewhere. Right. Kitty.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which it helps me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have two questions. And we have to do it quickly. Looking back, what is the connection between you as a child, a 16-year-old in the Holocaust, and you now? Is there a connection? Is there something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My Jewishness.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And my second question is relating to Ziggy, your friend who-- who came now and then, called you from the port. He was a German, I take it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a Nazi. And how did you form your relationship? What did it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a bankrupt Nazi and I was a bankrupt Jew. We were both fellow travelers. I joined Ankali. That is a movement means-- meaning-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --friend in Aborigine language, about AIDS sufferers. And you give support and warmth and companionship to a dying AIDS sufferer. Who was my first client?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anton. Anton was 42 when I met him. His father was not only in the SS, but he was a warden in the Warsaw ghetto. Anton has never seen a Jew in his life. And I knew that he was a Nazi. He was carried away by the Hitler films and stuff. And we formed, with hard work, because I deliver. They have a need. Every person has a need. And I have a gift. I deliver. And when he died in my arms, his dying wish was that when he's burned, his ashes shall be strewn over a Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Kitty, thank you very much for talking to us. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Rolling. Ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitty--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 28th of April, 1956, I went into labor. And that was my mother's birthday. And I said to the nurse, if I can have a baby tonight, till midnight, I'll give you a lovely present. Because it's my mother's birthday. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. I was in labor on the 28th. And on the 29th. And on the 30th. And at last, by cesarean section, my bundle of joy arrived. He was 9 and 1/2 kilos. On the 1st of May, my socialist baby. This is Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, Paul. PAUL (SON): Hi. Paul, did your mum tell you stories about the Holocaust when you were younger? PAUL (SON): Um-- no. No, not-- not really. I don't think we talked much about it till I was a teenager, until we lived together in New Guinea. Right. And was-- did that come out because of your prompting? Or did she decide to tell you? PAUL (SON): I think we-- we tended to speak a lot about our lives, our various lives. And that just came out as a natural part of that. I don't think we-- I don't think I specifically asked her what was-- what was life like during the Holocaust? I think we talked about it slowly, and gradually. Right. And, Kitty, what was it like for you? Did you have a definite idea of what you wanted to tell Paul? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wanted Paul to be Jewish. This was important to me. I wanted him to have a more Jewish background than I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very thrilled when we could go on [INAUDIBLE] together, to the temple. And Paul wasn't thrilled. He said, what are we doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knows us. Nobody says hello to us. This is a community.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are we doing here? This is hypocrisy. Over to you. Has it changed? Or is still hypocrisy? PAUL (SON): Oh, I-- I just found it-- I just found it boring. I don't think I found it hypocritical. I-- well, I've always been proud of my Jewish ethnicity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I've always been proud of it from an-- ethnic-- ethnicity-- ethnicity point of view. I don't think I've particularly remember being Jewish as a religion. But I love being Jewish. And I was certainly-- you know, I was-- I went to a Jewish school. And-- and I-- and I had-- I was Bar Mitzvahed and all that kind of stuff. But no, I don't think it's hypocritical. I-- I'm not-- I-- I mean, an example of a hypocrite is somebody who-- who goes to synagogue and doesn't believe. I mean, I do believe. But I don't particularly enjoy going to synagogue anymore. I don't really think it's-- it's me. I mean, I don't know. It's funny.

words.text: I've always been proud of it

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Your-- your mom has told us about some incredible experiences during the war. PAUL (SON): Right. Has that affected you, do you think? PAUL (SON): It's made me grateful that-- that I'm here. It's made me very fiercely proud of my heritage. The-- I've studied the Holocaust historically.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've done courses on it. And I think when I-- when I would do them, I treated the subject differently because of those experiences. And I would relate some of those stories. I remember when I was at New South Wales University. And I did a-- a tutorial, a very emotional tutorial, on the Holocaust. I would relate some of my mother's stories to the children, who were quite astounded. And-- and these stories are stories that I have handed down to people that I know and-- and to friends, and to students that I've taught over a great many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And Kitty, do you think the Holocaust and your experiences has affected your relationship with Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think more the marriage than the Holocaust. Because all I could salvage out of my misguided marriage was my son. And I love him dearly, because he's really all I've got in this whole wide world. Because my sister was killed in a car accident a few years ago. And I also think-- and Paul will tell you-- that it wasn't always very easy to have Kitty Fisher for a mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: PAUL (SON): That's true. What are the compensations? PAUL (SON): Oh, god. That's a-- that's a hard question to ask. I guess it's good to have a mother who is unique, who doesn't-- who is not afraid to-- to speak her mind, who is full of these stories which are-- are extraordinary stories about life and-- and past and present. And everything is sort of interwoven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the relationship has-- has gone through a number of developmental changes, I think, over the years. And there were times when-- I mean, I think we were-- I think the closest I was with her was actually when I was living with her in New Guinea. I think that was probably the happiest period of our lives, as-- as mother and son. But her compensations are that she's a unique individual and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Do you see things of her in yourself? PAUL (SON): We both eat too much. I think that's probably one of the-- one of the things. Um, I guess there must be. I mean, you have to inherit something from your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly what I've-- I think I've inherited her creativity. She's very good at-- at telling stories and writing. And that is my-- one of my major strengths.

speaker: interviewee
text: From her stories, I can see she was a real rebel. PAUL (SON): She still is, I think, yeah. Are you asking me if-- if I agree with that, or if I am a rebel? Or-- Whichever way you want to see it. PAUL (SON): [INAUDIBLE]. Ha! Um, no, I'm probably a lot more conservative-- Mhm. PAUL (SON): --than-- than she is. Although I'm certainly-- I guess I'm an individual. I mean, we're all individuals, to an extent. But no, I guess we-- I share that with her. But I'm certainly-- I-- I like to see myself as-- as having a certain uniqueness. Mm, she certainly is that. So, Kitty, after speaking today, and you've obviously had to think about your experiences now. Do you-- is there-- not a message, but something you would tell to Paul, and to future generations, whether you know, your grandchildren or other people's grandchildren from our community, about your experiences? Something that you brought away from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Share. Tell everybody and share. And try to protect them from making the same mistakes. That's what parenthood really is all about. I would like to know that Paul has a secure job, which I never had. I like to think, Paul, that he has such a wonderful hobby in his film review business, which gives him so much joy and contact with important people. I love his sense of humor, which when is somehow centered around me, is not so very funny anymore. And I love him, that he's here. And I am only sad that he's at 39, still alone. Because while I am around, and his father-- with whom he has a very good relationship-- is around, he's not alone. And I just would hate to think that he will have a lonely aging like I have.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, if there's nothing further you'd like to say-- PAUL (SON): I think that sounds fine. Good. Thanks very much. PAUL (SON): Thank you. OK, that's the end of tape five of the interview with Katarina Fisher. It's the 21st of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's the end of the whole interview. And it's stopped raining.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tape.
